title: SEGMENT 17 — Expanded access and the N-of-1 path — the milasen lessons

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Expanded access and the N-of-1 path — the milasen lessons

In Segment 1 I promised this lecture would intersect with the milasen story — the N-of-1 antisense oligonucleotide drug developed for a single patient with a unique mutation — and now we have arrived at the segment where it does.

Because the milasen case sits inside a regulatory framework called expanded access, sometimes called compassionate use, and this framework is one of the most important and underappreciated features of the modern FDA system.
The basic problem expanded access addresses is this.
Suppose you have a patient with a life-threatening illness.
There is a drug in development that might help them.
The drug is not yet approved.

The clinical trial that would eventually establish whether the drug works is enrolling, but the patient does not qualify, or the trial is full, or the patient cannot wait the years it will take for approval.
What do you do?
The expanded access framework provides a path for that patient to receive the unapproved drug outside the clinical trial, under specific conditions, with FDA oversight.
Three criteria generally apply.
First, the patient has a serious or immediately life-threatening illness.
Second, no comparable approved alternative is available.
Third, the potential benefit justifies the risk.
If those criteria are met, and if the manufacturer is willing to provide the drug, FDA can authorize expanded-access use.
There are three types of expanded access in current FDA practice.
Individual patient access — for a single named patient.
Intermediate-size patient population access — for a small cohort of patients.
Treatment IND — for a larger population access in advance of approval.

The mechanics differ slightly across the three types, but the underlying principle is the same: provide a path for patients with serious diseases to access investigational drugs when no other option exists.
For milasen, the situation was even more unusual than standard expanded access.
Mila Makovec had a completely unique mutation — a single SVA retrotransposon insertion in MFSD8 that had never been seen in any other patient.
There was no clinical trial of milasen because milasen had never existed; it was designed from scratch specifically for her.
The drug was conceived, manufactured, tested in animals, and submitted to FDA under an N-of-1 individual-patient IND in less than a year.
The FDA cleared the IND in record time.
Mila received her first dose.
The drug was administered intrathecally, into her cerebrospinal fluid.
The treatment stabilized parts of her clinical picture, including a substantial reduction in her seizures.
The regulatory significance of milasen extends beyond the single patient it was made for.
It established that the agency was willing to support, organizationally and regulatorily, the development of a drug for an individual.

It established that the timeline from molecular diagnosis to clinical dosing could be radically compressed for the right kind of intervention — in this case, a splice-switching antisense oligonucleotide.
And it raised, sharply and publicly, the question of what the right regulatory framework is for ultra-rare and N-of-1 medicines.
This question is now alive in the field.

The agency has been developing thinking about platform technology designations that would allow a master IND covering an entire class of related drugs — for example, all ASOs of a certain chemistry, or all CRISPR therapeutics for a defined class of targets — rather than requiring a separate IND for each individual sequence.
The idea is that the safety and pharmacology of the platform can be established once, and individual N-of-1 applications can ride on the platform with abbreviated regulatory submissions.
The platform approach is gaining traction.
It will, if it works, be transformative for the economics and timelines of N-of-1 medicine.
The milasen case is, in this sense, the proof of concept that the regulatory system can rise to meet the science.
It is also a reminder of the limits of that system.
As I mentioned in passing earlier — Mila Makovec died in 2021, at the age of ten.
The drug slowed her disease but did not save her life.

The agency's flexibility, the speed of the development, the brilliance of the molecular design — all of it produced an extraordinary medical achievement that nevertheless was not enough for this particular patient.
The expanded access pathway gave her a chance she would not otherwise have had.
That is what the pathway is for.
It is not a guarantee of benefit.
It is a regulatory mechanism for trying.
